segments.title: Builth at the River Wye crossing
segments.summary: The opening explains Builth Castle as a critical River Wye crossing between northern and southern Wales, even though little masonry survives.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke starts by making the ruined site legible through geography rather than surviving stonework. Builth sits at a critical River Wye crossing between important parts of northern and southern Wales. In 1277 Edward I regards the surrounding area as comparatively secure because local men are loyal to him and supply a substantial part of his forces. That loyalty also creates an obligation to protect their lands, so the crossing explains why a castle is needed in territory the king already considers friendly.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Builth Castle, notes that mostly earthworks survive, and identifies the River Wye crossing.
The transcript describes the crossing between northern and southern Wales, local loyalty to Edward, and the need to protect those lands.

segments.title: Marcher and Welsh claims before Edward I
segments.summary: The early-history section follows the older motte-and-bailey through Norman, Marcher, and Welsh ownership claims before its 1260 destruction.
segments.body: Before Edward I treats the site as a new project, Builth already has a complicated frontier history. Dr. Clarke describes an earlier motte-and-bailey with two baileys and another boundary wall. Norman, Marcher, and Welsh lords fight over it, intermarry, and sometimes base their claims on inheritance through marriage rather than on a simple ethnic division. The castle changes hands and is modified repeatedly before Llywelyn ap Gruffudd takes and destroys it in 1260 during Henry III's reign. The passage makes the Welsh Marches legible as a zone of overlapping lordship and family claims rather than a clean political boundary.

evidence.note: The transcript says Builth had a pre-existing motte-and-bailey castle with two baileys and a further boundary wall.
The transcript describes Norman, Marcher, and Welsh lords fighting, intermarrying, and claiming the castle by inheritance.
The transcript says Llywelyn ap Gruffudd took and destroyed the castle in 1260.

segments.title: Edward I's cheap unfinished rebuild
segments.summary: Edward I's rebuild treats Builth as a blank slate, reuses the earthworks to limit cost, and remains unfinished when the wider Welsh threat changes.
segments.body: This section explains why Builth did not become one of the showpiece Edwardian castles. Edward treats the destroyed predecessor as a blank slate and begins work in May 1277, but construction stops unfinished in August 1282 for lack of money. Llywelyn ap Gruffudd's death reduces the strategic pressure for a stronger work just as priorities shift toward Gwynedd. Reusing the existing earthworks holds the cost below 1,700 pounds, making Builth comparatively cheap. The sequence links castle design and completion directly to threat, budget, and the value of inherited terrain.

evidence.note: The transcript says Edward I considered the site a blank slate, began work in May 1277, and left the original castle unfinished in August 1282 for lack of money.
The transcript links the halt to reduced need after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd's death and says the existing earthworks kept costs under 1700 pounds.

segments.title: The unfinished castle proves defensible
segments.summary: Madog ap Llywelyn's revolt tests Builth in 1294, but the unfinished castle holds and remains useful enough for a royal grant and Roger Mortimer's castellanship.
segments.body: Builth's unfinished state does not make it militarily irrelevant. Forces in Madog ap Llywelyn's revolt besiege the castle in 1294 but do not take it. Edward I grants Builth to his son Edward in 1301 as part of organizing and supporting the territory. Roger Mortimer then serves as castellan from 1310 to 1315. This compact sequence shows a comparatively cheap fortification continuing to provide real defensive and administrative value.

evidence.note: The transcript says Madog ap Llywelyn's revolt besieged but did not take Builth, Edward granted it to his son in 1301, and Roger Mortimer served as castellan from 1310 to 1315.

segments.title: Mortimer, Isabella, and Edward III
segments.summary: Builth's ownership becomes entangled with Isabella, Roger Mortimer, Edward II's fall, and Edward III's recovery of royal authority.
segments.body: The castle's later story becomes court politics as much as Welsh frontier warfare. Edward II gives Builth to his wife Isabella, who leases it to Roger Mortimer and helps create the connection between them. Mortimer later becomes Isabella's lover and supports her coup against Edward II, which ends with the deposed king's death. When Edward III takes control as an adult in 1330, he captures Isabella and Mortimer, executes Mortimer for treason, and seizes his properties. Builth then passes to Gilbert Talbot, the Justiciar of South Wales, making the castle a marker of the regime change.

evidence.note: The transcript says Edward II gave the castle to Isabella, who leased it to Roger Mortimer, and that Mortimer became Isabella's lover.
The transcript links Mortimer and Isabella to the coup against Edward II, then describes Edward III's capture of them and Mortimer's execution.
The transcript says Edward III seized Mortimer's properties and Builth was taken by Gilbert Talbot, Justiciar of South Wales.

segments.title: Alice de Lacy and coerced lordship
segments.summary: Alice de Lacy's life grant and forced marriage to Hugh de Freyne show how personal vulnerability could redirect a strategically useful lordship.
segments.body: Edward III rewards Alice de Lacy and her second husband, Eubulus le Strange, after their assistance during his takeover. They receive Builth for life, linking the castle to loyalty at the start of the new reign. After Eubulus dies, Hugh de Freyne forces Alice into marriage and thereby gains recognition as lord of Builth. Dr. Clarke stresses that the act was not considered acceptable even then, but Alice lacked surviving family able to defend her and the king chose not to intervene. The episode exposes how inheritance, gendered vulnerability, and royal political calculation could redirect control of a castle.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Edward III's life grant to Alice de Lacy and Eubulus le Strange, Alice's coerced remarriage to Hugh de Freyne, the king's nonintervention, and Freyne's recognition as lord of Builth.

segments.title: The Black Prince's garrison right
segments.summary: The Black Prince rents Builth back to the Mortimers but retains wartime garrison rights later exercised during Owain Glyndwr's revolt.
segments.body: Alice de Lacy dies childless in 1348, allowing Edward III to grant Builth to the Black Prince. In 1359 the prince restores a rental arrangement with the still-powerful Mortimer family but keeps the right to garrison the castle in wartime. That retained military authority matters when Owain Glyndwr's forces attack in the early fifteenth century. The garrison is commanded by Sir John Oldcastle, whom Dr. Clarke identifies as an inspiration for Shakespeare's Falstaff. Repairs costing 400 pounds in 1409 show the crown still spending heavily enough to keep the site serviceable after the crisis.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the grant to the Black Prince, the Mortimer rental with retained wartime garrison rights, Glyndwr's attack, Sir John Oldcastle's command, and the 1409 repair.

segments.title: Builth returns to the Tudor crown
segments.summary: The Wars of the Roses end the Mortimer rental, while Yorkist inheritance and Elizabeth of York's marriage carry Builth back into Tudor royal hands.
segments.body: The final Mortimer rental ends when Richard of York claims the crown during the Wars of the Roses. The deaths and inheritances that follow eventually make Elizabeth of York the relevant heir. Her marriage to the Lancastrian claimant Henry VII brings Builth back into royal hands after the dynastic conflict. In 1493 the site is re-established as a Marcher lordship for their son Arthur. Arthur's childless death in 1502 closes another short-lived attempt to anchor the lordship in a royal household.

evidence.note: The transcript ends the Mortimer rental during the Wars of the Roses, follows Elizabeth of York's marriage to Henry VII, and describes the Marcher lordship created for Arthur in 1493 before his childless death.

segments.title: Fire and stone robbing erase the castle
segments.summary: Fire during Henry VIII's reign and local reuse of the ruins explain why Builth survives mainly as earthworks rather than visible masonry.
segments.body: Builth's disappearance is a historical process rather than evidence that no substantial castle stood here. The structure burns during Henry VIII's reign. Local people then quarry the ruins for building stone, transferring its masonry into nearby properties. By the time of the video no castle stonework remains visible, which is why photographs, terrain, and earthworks must carry the interpretation. This concise ending connects Tudor decline to the practical problem of presenting the site to modern visitors.

evidence.note: The transcript says the castle burned during Henry VIII's reign, local people reused the ruins as building material, and no stonework now survives.

segments.title: A defensive castle at the edge of control
segments.summary: The closing argument treats Builth as the inland exception that clarifies the Ring of Iron: a river-edge defensive site protecting already controlled land.
segments.body: Builth is useful precisely because it is not an obvious maritime castle. Dr. Clarke presents its inland location as a challenge to the series' sea-power argument, then points back to the large river and the edge of English-controlled territory. Its job is more defensive than offensive: it protects the part of Wales already held rather than serving as the principal means of conquering territory beyond it. Once that controlled area becomes secure, the need for further investment falls away. The comparison helps viewers distinguish a frontier shield and communications point from the coastal strongholds used to project power and receive supply by sea.

evidence.note: The transcript says Builth is far from the sea, a challenge to the maritime-power argument, but still at the edge of a large river and controlled territory.
The transcript says the castle is more defensive than offensive and is about defending the part of Wales already controlled.
The transcript jokes about the castle's unlucky ownership history after explaining its defensive purpose.
